content.text: Management Group Structure

The ESLZ management group hierarchy is the structural backbone of the entire platform. Every policy, RBAC role assignment, and cost boundary in the landing zone is anchored to a management group scope. The hierarchy has five tiers: Tenant Root, the ALZ root (alz), four second-tier classification groups (platform, landing-zones, sandbox, decommissioned), three third-tier platform sub-groups (connectivity, identity, management), and two third-tier workload classifications (corp, online). Subscriptions live at the leaves and inherit everything above them.

attrs.alt: ESLZ management group hierarchy with policy inheritance

content.text: Management groups are scope, not containers. Moving a subscription between management groups changes which policies and role assignments it inherits — it does not move any resources. This makes the hierarchy the primary governance control plane.
Tier-by-Tier Rationale

Each tier in the hierarchy serves a specific governance purpose. The separation is not organizational — it is technical. Different tiers need different policy sets, and collapsing tiers into one forces policy exceptions that erode governance quality.
MG
Purpose
Policy posture
Who manages subscriptions here
alz (root)
Single scope for cross-cutting policies (Defender, diagnostics, allowed regions)
Audit/Modify effects only; no Deny at this level to avoid false lockouts
Platform team (subscription vending service)
alz-platform
Isolate platform infrastructure from workload governance
Stricter Deny on public endpoints for platform resources
Platform team only — no application team access
alz-platform-connectivity
Connectivity subscription: hub VNet, Firewall, ExpressRoute, DNS
Deny changes to hub VNet address space without approval
Network platform engineers
alz-platform-identity
Identity subscription: AD DS, Entra ID Domain Services, PIM config
Deny internet exposure on identity resources
Identity platform engineers
alz-platform-management
Management subscription: Log Analytics, Defender workspace, Automation
Deny deletion of monitoring resources (resource lock equivalent via Policy)
Platform engineers
alz-landing-zones
Parent of all application landing zones
Require NSG on subnets, require private endpoints for PaaS
Platform team (scope); application teams manage their subscriptions
alz-corp
Workloads that must not be internet-facing (internal APIs, databases, ERP)
Deny public IPs, deny inbound internet paths
Application teams within their subscription; no MG-level access
alz-online
Workloads that require internet access (public web apps, APIs, CDN)
Allow App Gateway, Azure Front Door; deny direct VM internet access
Application teams within their subscription
alz-sandbox
Developer and innovation experimentation
Relaxed: allow public IPs, no NSG requirement, cost budget alert only
Individual developers; auto-expire subscriptions after 90 days
alz-decommissioned
Retiring subscriptions awaiting deletion
Deny all new resource operations; read-only access only
Platform team — no application team access after migration
Creating the Hierarchy with the Azure CLI

Management groups are tenant-level resources; the commands run against the tenant, not a subscription. The operator needs the Management Group Contributor role at the Tenant Root. In large organizations, this is a privileged role held by the platform team and activated via PIM.

# Create the full ESLZ management group hierarchy
# Run with Owner/Contributor on the Tenant Root MG

TENANT_ROOT=$(az account management-group list \
  --query '[?type==`/`].name' -o tsv 2>/dev/null || az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups?api-version=2020-05-01' \
  --query 'value[?properties.tenantId==properties.tenantId && !properties.parent].name' -o tsv | head -1)

echo "Tenant root MG: $TENANT_ROOT"

# Tier 1: ALZ root
az account management-group create --name alz --display-name 'Azure Landing Zones' \
  --parent $TENANT_ROOT

# Tier 2: four top-level classifications
az account management-group create --name alz-platform       --display-name 'Platform'        --parent alz
az account management-group create --name alz-landing-zones  --display-name 'Landing Zones'   --parent alz
az account management-group create --name alz-sandbox        --display-name 'Sandbox'         --parent alz
az account management-group create --name alz-decommissioned --display-name 'Decommissioned'  --parent alz

# Tier 3 under alz-platform
az account management-group create --name alz-platform-connectivity --display-name 'Connectivity' --parent alz-platform
az account management-group create --name alz-platform-identity     --display-name 'Identity'     --parent alz-platform
az account management-group create --name alz-platform-management   --display-name 'Management'   --parent alz-platform

# Tier 3 under alz-landing-zones
az account management-group create --name alz-corp   --display-name 'Corp'   --parent alz-landing-zones
az account management-group create --name alz-online --display-name 'Online'  --parent alz-landing-zones

# Verify hierarchy
az account management-group show --name alz --expand --recurse \
  --query '{Name:name, Children:children[].{Name:name, Children:children[].name}}' -o json
Placing Subscriptions in the Hierarchy

A subscription always belongs to exactly one management group. Placing it in the right group at creation time is critical because policy assignments take effect immediately — a subscription that enters alz-corp inherits the Deny public IP policy before any resources are deployed. The subscription vending automation handles placement as part of the provisioning workflow.

# Move platform subscriptions into their dedicated MGs
# (subscriptions must already exist)

SUB_CONN='<connectivity-sub-id>'
SUB_IDNT='<identity-sub-id>'
SUB_MGMT='<management-sub-id>'

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-platform-connectivity \
  --subscription $SUB_CONN

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-platform-identity \
  --subscription $SUB_IDNT

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-platform-management \
  --subscription $SUB_MGMT

# Assign a workload subscription to the corp landing zone MG
# (done once per workload; usually automated via subscription vending)
SUB_PAYMENTS='<payments-sub-id>'
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-corp \
  --subscription $SUB_PAYMENTS

# Assign a sandbox subscription (one per team)
SUB_SANDBOX='<team-sandbox-sub-id>'
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-sandbox \
  --subscription $SUB_SANDBOX

# Decommission a subscription: move it to the decommissioned MG
# The Deny-all policy on this MG blocks any new resource creation.
SUB_RETIRE='<retiring-sub-id>'
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name alz-decommissioned \
  --subscription $SUB_RETIRE

# List all subscriptions in the hierarchy
az account management-group show \
  --name alz --expand --recurse \
  --query 'children[].{MG:displayName,Subscriptions:children[?type=="/subscriptions"].displayName}' \
  -o table
Policy Assignments by MG Tier

Policy assignments cascade down the hierarchy. A policy assigned at alz applies to every subscription under alz-platform, alz-landing-zones, alz-sandbox, and alz-decommissioned simultaneously. Each tier below adds the policies that are specific to its classification. The effective policy set on any subscription is the union of all policies from the Tenant Root down to the subscription's management group.

# Assign baseline policies at the correct MG scope for each tier

# --- ALZ root: applies to ALL subscriptions in the hierarchy ---
ALZ_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz'

# Enable Defender for Cloud across all plans
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'enable-mdfc-alz' \
  --display-name 'Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud (ALZ scope)' \
  --policy-set-definition '1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8' \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned \
  --location eastus

# Route diagnostic settings to central Log Analytics workspace
LOG_WS='/subscriptions/<mgmt-sub>/resourceGroups/rg-management-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/log-management-prod-eastus'
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'diag-settings-alz' \
  --display-name 'Deploy diagnostic settings to central workspace' \
  --policy '6f8f98a4-f108-47cb-8e98-a527f8bf5a75' \
  --scope $ALZ_SCOPE \
  --params "{\"logAnalytics\":{\"value\":\"$LOG_WS\"}}" \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned \
  --location eastus

# --- alz-landing-zones: applies to all workload subscriptions ---
LZ_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz-landing-zones'

# Require NSG on every subnet
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-nsg-subnets' \
  --display-name 'Subnets should be associated with a Network Security Group' \
  --policy 'e37b3640-1a59-41c0-b21e-9b6e7d1f9a91' \
  --scope $LZ_SCOPE

# --- alz-corp: deny internet-facing resources ---
CORP_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz-corp'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-public-ip-corp' \
  --display-name 'Deny public IP addresses in Corp landing zones' \
  --policy '83a86a26-fd1f-447c-b59d-e51f44264114' \
  --scope $CORP_SCOPE \
  --enforcement-mode Default

# --- alz-decommissioned: deny all new deployments ---
DECOM_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz-decommissioned'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-all-decom' \
  --display-name 'Deny all resource operations in decommissioned subscriptions' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/a77a1ef0-29a9-4a85-8a53-d82bc4e1f2fd' \
  --scope $DECOM_SCOPE \
  --enforcement-mode Default
MG Hierarchy Design Rules
Rule
Rationale
Anti-pattern to avoid
Keep the hierarchy at 5 levels or fewer
Each additional level adds complexity to policy scope analysis and role assignment auditing
Creating a MG per business unit (10+ MGs at tier 2) that mirrors the org chart
Never assign subscriptions directly to the Tenant Root
Subscriptions at the root bypass ALZ governance and have no isolation
Leaving the default subscription (from account creation) at the root
Separate platform and workload subscriptions at tier 2
Platform resources have different policy requirements (stricter on some axes, different on others)
Putting the connectivity subscription in alz-landing-zones with workloads
Use alz-sandbox for ALL experimentation
Sandbox provides cost guardrails and policy relaxation without compromising workload MGs
Applying exceptions to alz-corp policies for a 'dev mode' workload
Move retiring subscriptions to alz-decommissioned before deletion
The Deny-all policy prevents accidental new deployments during the wind-down period
Deleting a subscription without a decommissioning period (risks losing audit logs)
Assign policies at the highest applicable scope
A policy assigned at alz-landing-zones covers all future corp and online subscriptions automatically
Assigning policies per-subscription — new subscriptions are ungoverned until manually assigned
RBAC at Management Group Scope

Role assignments at management group scope are inherited by every subscription and resource group below them. This makes MG-scope role assignments powerful but risky — a Contributor role assigned at alz-platform propagates to the connectivity, identity, and management subscriptions simultaneously. The correct model is: platform team gets Reader at the ALZ root (visibility), specialized teams get role assignments on their specific child MG, and application teams never get MG-level write access — only subscription-scope access to their workload subscription.

# RBAC assignments at management group scope
# Role assignments at a MG scope are inherited by all subscriptions below.
# Use this carefully — a Contributor at alz can modify any workload subscription.

# Grant the platform team Reader at the ALZ root (for visibility)
PLATFORM_TEAM_OID='<platform-team-aad-group-oid>'
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Reader' \
  --assignee-object-id $PLATFORM_TEAM_OID \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz'

# Grant the network team Contributor on the connectivity MG only
NETWORK_TEAM_OID='<network-team-aad-group-oid>'
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Network Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id $NETWORK_TEAM_OID \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz-platform-connectivity'

# Grant application teams Contributor on their subscription (not the MG)
# Workload teams should NEVER have MG-level write access
APP_TEAM_OID='<payments-team-aad-group-oid>'
SUB_PAYMENTS='<payments-sub-id>'
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --assignee-object-id $APP_TEAM_OID \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$SUB_PAYMENTS"

# Audit: list all role assignments at the ALZ root MG
az role assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz' \
  --include-inherited \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Principal:principalName, Scope:scope}' \
  -o table

# Detect standing Owner/Contributor at MG scope (should be eligible PIM only)
az role assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz' \
  --query '[?roleDefinitionName==`Owner` || roleDefinitionName==`Contributor`]' \
  --query '[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Principal:principalName, Scope:scope}' \
  -o table
Protecting the Hierarchy from Accidental Changes

Azure does not support resource locks on management groups. Protection is implemented through Azure Policy (Deny effect on management group deletion actions) and the tenant-level hierarchy settings (requireAuthorizationForGroupCreation blocks creation of new management groups without explicit permission). Setting the defaultManagementGroup to alz-sandbox ensures any subscription that loses its MG assignment lands in the sandbox — not at the Tenant Root — where it inherits the sandbox policy set rather than no policy.

# Resource locks on management groups are NOT natively supported in Azure.
# Instead, use Azure Policy with Deny effect to protect critical MG-level settings.

# Policy: deny deletion of management groups under the ALZ root
# This uses a custom policy definition with a Deny effect on the delete action.

az policy definition create \
  --name 'deny-mg-deletion' \
  --display-name 'Deny deletion of ESLZ management groups' \
  --mode All \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "allOf": [
        { "field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Management/managementGroups" },
        {
          "field": "name",
          "in": ["alz","alz-platform","alz-landing-zones","alz-corp","alz-online",
                  "alz-sandbox","alz-decommissioned","alz-platform-connectivity",
                  "alz-platform-identity","alz-platform-management"]
        }
      ]
    },
    "then": { "effect": "Deny" }
  }' \
  --subscription '<mgmt-sub-id>'

# Assign to tenant root to prevent deletion from any scope
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-mg-deletion' \
  --display-name 'Deny deletion of ESLZ management groups' \
  --policy 'deny-mg-deletion' \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<tenant-root-mg-id>'

# Enable the MG hierarchy settings (require authorization for MG creation)
# This prevents anyone from creating rogue MGs at the tenant root
az rest \
  --method PUT \
  --uri 'https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<tenant-root-mg-id>/settings?api-version=2021-04-01' \
  --body '{
    "properties": {
      "requireAuthorizationForGroupCreation": true,
      "defaultManagementGroup": "alz-sandbox"
    }
  }'
# defaultManagementGroup ensures new subscriptions go to the sandbox MG
# instead of floating at the Tenant Root when no explicit MG is assigned.
Auditing the Hierarchy with Resource Graph

Resource Graph can enumerate management groups, verify subscription placement, count subscriptions per MG leaf, and surface standing privileged role assignments at MG scope. These queries form the backbone of a weekly governance health report. Query 2 (subscriptions outside the ALZ root) should return zero rows in a healthy estate — any result indicates a subscription created outside the vending workflow.

// Resource Graph: audit management group structure and subscription placement

// 1. All management groups in the tenant with their parent chain
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.management/managementgroups'
| extend
    parentName = tostring(properties.details.parent.displayName),
    level      = array_length(managementGroupAncestorsChain)
| project name, displayName, parentName, level
| order by level asc, name asc

// 2. Subscriptions not under the ALZ root (should return zero rows in a healthy estate)
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| where managementGroupAncestorsChain !has 'alz'
| project name, subscriptionId, managementGroupAncestorsChain

// 3. Count subscriptions per management group leaf
ResourceContainers
| where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
| mv-expand ancestors = managementGroupAncestorsChain
| extend mgName = tostring(ancestors.name)
| where mgName in ('alz-corp','alz-online','alz-sandbox','alz-decommissioned',
    'alz-platform-connectivity','alz-platform-identity','alz-platform-management')
| summarize SubscriptionCount = dcount(subscriptionId) by mgName
| order by SubscriptionCount desc

// 4. Role assignments at MG scope (detect standing privileged access)
AuthorizationResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/roleassignments'
| where tostring(properties.scope) contains '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups'
| extend
    principalId  = tostring(properties.principalId),
    roleDefId    = tostring(properties.roleDefinitionId),
    scope        = tostring(properties.scope)
| where roleDefId endswith '8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635'  // Owner
    or roleDefId endswith 'b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'  // Contributor
| project principalId, roleDefId, scope
| order by scope asc

// 5. Policy compliance by management group
PolicyResources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.policyinsights/policystates'
| where tostring(properties.complianceState) =~ 'NonCompliant'
| extend mgScope = tostring(properties.managementGroupIds)
| summarize NonCompliant = count() by mgScope
| order by NonCompliant desc
Renaming and Migrating Management Groups

The management group display name can be changed freely. The management group ID (the name field used in ARM IDs and policy scope strings) is immutable — renaming it requires creating a new MG, moving all subscriptions, reassigning all policies and role assignments, and deleting the old MG. The migration must be sequenced carefully: create new MG, move subscriptions, recreate policy/RBAC assignments on new scope, delete old MG. Deleting the old MG before recreating policy assignments leaves subscriptions ungoverned for the gap.

# Rename a management group (display name only — the 'name' (ID) is immutable)
# Changing the name/ID requires creating a new MG and moving subscriptions.

# 1. Change display name (safe, no policy disruption)
az account management-group update \
  --name alz-corp \
  --display-name 'Internal Workloads'

# 2. Rename the MG ID (requires a migration): example corp -> alz-internal
# Step A: create the new MG under the same parent
az account management-group create \
  --name alz-internal \
  --display-name 'Internal Workloads' \
  --parent alz-landing-zones

# Step B: move all subscriptions from old MG to new MG
for SUB in $(az account management-group show \
              --name alz-corp --expand \
              --query 'children[?type=="/subscriptions"].name' -o tsv); do
  az account management-group subscription add \
    --name alz-internal \
    --subscription $SUB
  az account management-group subscription remove \
    --name alz-corp \
    --subscription $SUB
  echo "Moved $SUB from alz-corp to alz-internal"
done

# Step C: reassign policies from old MG to new MG
# Identify policy assignments on the old MG
az policy assignment list \
  --scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/alz-corp' \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Policy:policyDefinitionId}' -o table

# Recreate each assignment on the new MG scope, then delete from old MG
# (Rebuild role assignments similarly)

# Step D: delete the old empty MG
az account management-group delete --name alz-corp

echo 'Migration complete: alz-corp -> alz-internal'
Common Management Group Mistakes
Mistake
What breaks
Correct approach
Creating MGs that mirror org chart (department per MG)
Policy requirements don't map to org structure; policy exceptions accumulate as orgs change
Structure by workload classification (corp/online) and platform role, not organizational hierarchy
Assigning Owner at the ALZ root to the platform team
Any team member can modify any subscription in the estate; no audit trail when privilege is used
Use PIM eligible assignments at the MG scope; require justification for activation
Putting sandbox subscriptions in alz-corp
Sandbox relaxations (allow public IPs) must be implemented as exceptions on the corp policy set
alz-sandbox has its own relaxed policy set; sandbox subs go there
Not setting defaultManagementGroup
New subscriptions land at the Tenant Root, outside all ALZ policies, until manually moved
Set defaultManagementGroup = alz-sandbox in tenant hierarchy settings
Creating more than 3 levels below the ALZ root
Policy scope analysis becomes intractable; role assignment audit is unmanageable
Keep the hierarchy flat: root → classification → (optional) sub-classification → subscriptions
Assigning policies per subscription instead of per MG
Every new subscription is ungoverned until the platform team manually assigns policies
Assign at the MG scope; per-subscription assignments are only for workload-specific overrides
Summary

The ESLZ management group hierarchy is a five-tier tree where each tier encodes a governance boundary: tenant root for tenant-wide baselines, alz for ALZ-wide audit and monitoring policies, alz-platform for isolated platform infrastructure, alz-landing-zones for application workload policies split into corp (internal) and online (internet-facing) classifications, alz-sandbox for developer experimentation, and alz-decommissioned for retiring subscriptions. Policy assignments at the right tier scope eliminate per-subscription policy management and ensure every new subscription is governed from its first deployment.
